  • Tutorials for the end user »

Language - validating data¶

It is difficult to find useful examples of a language while keeping them simple. For a full reference for the language please see Language definition. Lets try to define the language we gave as an example in the Introduction of the Language module.

Simple example¶

We can start with a very simple definition of the keywords (Example 1):

from semperpy.language.language import Language

#-----------------------------------------------------------
# The language
#-----------------------------------------------------------
retrieve = dict(
    directive = "retrieve",
    keywords = dict(
        date = dict(
        ),
        step = dict(
        ),
        parameter = dict(
        ),
        level = dict(
        ),
        experiment_version = dict(
        )
    )
)

def reader(s):
    return retrieve

directive = dict(
    date = 2020101012,
    step = ['24','49'],
    parameter = "geopotential",
    level = [500,1000],
    experiment_version = ["0012","0013"],
)
print Language.resolveDirective(directive, "retrieve", reader)

When we run the program we get:

{'date': [2020101012], 'experiment_version': ['0012'], 'parameter': ['geopotential'], 'step': [24, 48], 'level': [500, 1000]}

So so far we have a tool which returns the dictionary we submit to it, it’s a start. However, if we run:

directive = dict(
    date = 2020101012,
    step = [24,48],
    parameter = "geopotential",
    level = [500,1000],
    experiment_version = "0012",
    type = "forecast"
)

we get:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "langdef1.py", line 33, in <module>
    print Language.resolveDirective(directive, "retrieve", reader)
  File "Language/Language.py", line 135, in resolveDirective
    error.check()
  File "Core/tools.py", line 161, in check
    raise self.exception('\n' + '\n'.join(self.msg))
Language.Language.LanguageError:
Unknow keyword 'type'

At least there is already some control on the validity of dictionary we submit to be checked against the language. If we run:

directive = dict(
    date = 2020101012,
    step = ['24','49'],
    parameter = "geopotential",
    level = [500,1000],
    experiment_version = ["0012","0013"],
)

{'date': [2020101012], 'experiment_version': ['0012', '0013'], 'parameter': ['geopotential'], 'step': ['24', '49'], 'level': [500, 1000]}

Specifying more¶

There are four problems in Example 1.

  • we would like the steps to be integers and not strings and we don’t want to depend on the way these are input by the user
  • we know that we will not find step 49 in the archive, we might as well try to get rid of wrong requests before starting to read / retrieve data.
  • we would like to limit a request to one single experiment version in order to limit the size of the data which is returned.
  • experiment_version is a long name, it could be useful to use something like: ev or expver.

We can cater for these as in Example 2:

from semperpy.language.language import Language

retrieve = dict(
    directive = "retrieve",
    keywords = dict(
        date = dict(
            # dates will be converted to integer
            type = int,
        ),
        step = dict(
            # steps will be converted to integer
            type = int,
        ),
        parameter = dict(
        ),
        level = dict(
        ),
        experiment_version = dict(
            # you can now use ev or expver as well as experiment_version
            # but you can only specify one of these
            unique = True,
            alias = ["ev","expver"],
        )
    )
)

def reader(s):
    return retrieve

directive = dict(
    date = 2020101012,
    step = ['24','49'],
    parameter = "geopotential",
    level = [500,1000],
    expver = "0012",
)
print Language.resolveDirective(directive, "retrieve", reader)

When we run the program we now get:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "langdef2.py", line 37, in <module>
    print Language.resolveDirective(directive, "retrieve", reader)
  File "Language/Language.py", line 135, in resolveDirective
    error.check()
  File "Core/tools.py", line 161, in check
    raise self.exception('\n' + '\n'.join(self.msg))
Language.Language.LanguageError:
The keyword 'experiment_version' only takes one value

So we are now protected against the input of too many values. Please note that the message displays experiment_version and not exvper which was input. This is because the alias was replaced with the real name of the keyword.

If we now run:

directive = dict(
    date = 2020101012,
    step = ['24','49'],
    parameter = "geopotential",
    level = [500,1000],
    expver = "0012",
)

{'level': [500, 1000], 'experiment_version': '0012', 'step': [24, 49], 'date': [2020101012], 'parameter': ['geopotential']}

Validators¶

The steps are now integers and expver is converted to experiment_version. We still need to cater for step 49, Example 3:

note:We specified here type int, but any existing Python class would work as well. 
from semperpy.language.language import Language

#----------------------------------------------------
# This is how we validate the values of steps, see
# how it is parameterised in the language definition
# values is always passed as a list
#----------------------------------------------------
def ValidateChoice(directive,keyword,values,*args):
    for value in values:
        if not value in args:
            raise ValueError("for keyword %s. The value: '%s' is not in the set: %s" % (keyword, value, ', '.join([str(x) for x in args])))
    return values

retrieve = dict(
    directive = "retrieve",
    keywords = dict(
        date = dict(
            # dates will be converted to integer
            type = int,
        ),
        step = dict(
            # steps will be converted to integer
            type = int,
            validate = [ValidateChoice,24,48,72,96,120,144,168]
        ),
        parameter = dict(
        ),
        level = dict(
        ),
        experiment_version = dict(
            # you can now use ev or expver as well as experiment_version
            # but you can only specify one of these
            unique = True,
            alias = ["ev","expver"],
        )
    )
)

def reader(s):
    return retrieve

directive = dict(
    date = 2020101012,
    step = ['24','49'],
    parameter = "geopotential",
    level = [500,1000],
    expver = "0012",
)
print Language.resolveDirective(directive, "retrieve", reader)

When we run this:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "langdef3.py", line 49, in <module>
    print Language.resolveDirective(directive, "retrieve", reader)
  File "Language/Language.py", line 157, in resolveDirective
    error.check()
  File "Core/tools.py", line 161, in check
    raise self.exception('\n' + '\n'.join(self.msg))
Language.Language.LanguageError:
Validation error for keyword step. The value: '49' is not in the set: 24, 48, 72, 96, 120, 144, 168

Inheritance¶

It is sometimes useful to define a language in different chunks which can be reused by different languages, this is basically called modularity. Please see the following example, although it is not extremely useful, it shows how to use inheritance (Example 4):

from semperpy.language.language import Language

parameter = dict(
    directive = "parameter",
    keywords = dict(
        parameter = dict(
        ),
        level = dict(
            type = int,
            default_value = 500,
            optional = True,
        ),
    )
)

date = dict(
    directive = "date",
    keywords = dict(
        date = dict(
            type = int,
        ),
        step = dict(
            type = int,
        ),
        level = dict(
            type = str,
        )
    )
)

retrieve = dict(
    directive = "retrieve",
    inherit_from = ["parameter","date"],
    keywords = dict(
        experiment_version = dict(
            unique = True,
            alias = "expver"
        ),
        target = dict(
        )
    )
)

def reader(s):
    dirs = dict(
        parameter = parameter,
        date = date,
        retrieve = retrieve
    )
    return dirs[s]

directive = dict(
    date = 2020101012,
    step = [24,48],
    parameter = "geopotential",
    level = [500,1000],
    experiment_version = "0012",
    target = "myFile"
)
print Language.resolveDirective(directive, "retrieve", reader)
note:Inheritance is a very useful tool, a language can inherit the definition of multiple languages. The inheritance is carried out from left to right with precedence to the left: in date, the definition of the keyword level has no effect, because the definition of level in parameter has precedence.

It may happen that you want to inherit from a language definition, but do not want to inherit all keywords. You can get rid of the unwanted keywords as follows:

from semperpy.language.language import Language

a = dict(
    directive = "a",
    keywords = dict(
        hello = dict(
        ),
        world = dict(
        )
    )
)

b = dict(
    directive = "b",
    inherit_from = "a",
    keywords = dict(
        world = dict(
        remove = True
        )
    )
)

def reader(s):
    if s == "a":
        return a
    else:
        return b

directive = dict(
    hello = 1,
    world = 2
)

print Language.resolveDirective(directive, "a", reader)
print Language.resolveDirective(directive, "b", reader)

{'world': [2], 'hello': [1]}

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "t.py", line 35, in <module>
    print Language.resolveDirective(directive, "b", reader)
  File "Language/Language.py", line 142, in resolveDirective
    error.check()
  File "Core/tools.py", line 161, in check
    raise self.exception('\n' + '\n'.join(self.msg))
Language.Language.LanguageError:
Unknown keyword 'world'

The first call succeeds while the second one fails, because the keyword world is undefined in the b language.

Specialization¶

It is also useful to be able to specify different definitions of the language depending on some of the values supplied by the user. Depending on the value of one of the entries in the directive, we may want to change some definitions, change default values or add or remove keywords. This is analoguous to dynamic inheritance in object-oriented programming. This is defined using the keyword specialize_from (Example 5):

from semperpy.core.tools import dictionary
from semperpy.language.language import Language

field_directive = dict(
    directive = "field",
    specialize_from = {
        "source == 'merra'": "merra",
        "parameter == 'mslp' or parameter == 't2m'" : "surface"
    },
    keywords = dict(
        source = dict(
            type = str,
        ),
        type = dict(
            type = str,
            optional = True,
            default_value = "fc" # forecast
        ),
        level = dict(
            type = int,
            optional = True,
            default_value = 500, # 500 hPa
        ),
        leveltype = dict(
            type = str,
            optional = True,
            default_value = "pl" # pressure level
        ),
        parameter = dict(
            type = str,
        )
    )
)

surface_directive = dict(
    directive = "surface",
    keywords = dict(
        level = dict(
            remove = True
        ),
        leveltype = dict(
            type = str,
            optional = True,
            default_value = "sfc" # surface level
        ),
    )
)

merra_directive = dict(
    directive = "merra",
    keywords = dict(
        type = dict(
            type = str,
            optional = True,
            default_value = "an" # analysis
        ),
        dataset = dict(
            type = str
        )
    )
)

def reader(s):
    dirs = dict(
        field   = field_directive,
        surface = surface_directive,
        merra   = merra_directive,
    )
    return dirs[s]

directive = dict(
    source = 'oper',
    parameter = 't2m',
)
print dictionary(Language.resolveDirective(directive, "field", reader))

What we are doing here is to define a default language for a directive comprising:

  • source
  • type
  • level
  • leveltype
  • parameter

There are default values for some of the keywords. We decide to automatically accommodate some special cases. When the source is merra we want to force the type to “an” (analysis) and we add a new keyword dataset. Whenever the parameter is a surface parameter we want to force the level type to surface and remove the keyword level.

If we try this language definition:

directive = dict(
    source = 'oper',
    level = 500,
    parameter = 'gh',
)
print Language.resolveDirective(directive, "field", reader)
-->
    source:
            oper
    parameter:
            gh
    leveltype:
            pl
    type:
            fc
    level:
            500

Now we change the source and walk though different errors:

directive = dict(
    source = 'merra',
    level = 500,
    parameter = 'gh',
)
print dictionary(Language.resolveDirective(directive, "field", reader))
--> Keyword dataset is missing and is required

The fact that we introduced the source as being merra, we inserted a new required keyword dataset so the system rightfully complains. If we correct that:

directive = dict(
    source = 'merra',
    level = 500,
    parameter = 'gh',
    dataset = 'MAI3CPASM'
)
print dictionary(Language.resolveDirective(directive, "field", reader))
-->
    source:
            merra
    level:
            500
    type:
            an
    leveltype:
            pl
    parameter:
            gh
    dataset:
            mai3cpasm

We now change the parameter to t2m (two meter temperature):

directive = dict(
    source = 'merra',
    level = 500,
    parameter = 't2m',
    dataset = 'MAI3CPASM'
)
print dictionary(Language.resolveDirective(directive, "field", reader))
--> Unknown keyword 'level'

The fact that we introduce a surface parameter removes the keyword level, so the system, once again, rightfully complains. When we fix it:

directive = dict(
     source = 'merra',
     parameter = 't2m',
     dataset = 'MAI3CPASM'
 )
 print dictionary(Language.resolveDirective(directive, "field", reader))
 -->
     source:
             merra
     parameter:
             t2m
     leveltype:
             sfc
     type:
             an
     dataset:
             mai3cpasm

If we change the source back to oper:

directive = dict(
    source = 'oper',
    parameter = 't2m',
    dataset = 'MAI3CPASM'
)
print dictionary(Language.resolveDirective(directive, "field", reader))
--> Unknown keyword 'dataset'

The dataset keyword is not valid anymore. Let’s remove it:

directive = dict(
    source = 'oper',
    parameter = 't2m',
)
print dictionary(Language.resolveDirective(directive, "field", reader))
-->
    source:
            oper
    leveltype:
            sfc
    parameter:
            t2m
    type:
            fc

And we are back to a valid directive.

Language format¶

So far we have shown examples using Python dictionaries to define languages. This is the format which is expected eventually by the Language module, but defining languages in the actual Python language may not be the best way of doing it. If for some reason a language definition needed to be shared with another language this wouldn’t be very practical. Pretty much any file format can be used to store language definition information provided that the reader converts the file to the expected dictionary.

At this stage it seems like a good idea to use json which is used heavily in web technologies to store the language definitions. It has some advantages: * it looks very much like Python, * decoding it is fast and straightforward, * decoders are available for many languages. XML could also be used.

Here is an example of how a language can be written in json (Example 6):

{
    "directive" : "retrieve",
    "keywords" : {
        "date": {},
        "step": {
            "type": "int",
            "validate": ["ValidateChoice",24,48,72,96,120,144,168]
        },
        "parameter": {
            "alias" : "param"
        },
        "level": {},
        "experiment_version": {
            "unique": true
        }
    }
}

The Python program then becomes:

from semperpy.language.language import Language
from semperpy.core.json import JSon

def ValidateChoice(directive,keyword,values,*args):
    for value in values:
        if not value in args:
                raise ValueError("for keyword %s. The value: '%s' is not in the set: %s" % (keyword, value, ', '.join([str(x) for x in args])))
    return values

def reader(name):
    f = open(name + ".json","r") # a configuration path could be used
    s = JSon.decode(f)
    f.close()
    return s

directive = dict(
    date = 2020101012,
    step = [24,48],
    param = "geopotential",
    level = [500,1000],
    experiment_version = "0012",
)
print Language.resolveDirective(directive, "retrieve", reader)

The syntax in json is a little more cumbersome than in Python, but having configuration files which are not in the language used is a good thing. Please note that both the type int and the function ValidateChoice are written as strings. The Language finds them at run time (if they are defined).
